title: Matter & Materials
objective: Students will explore matter & materials concepts including States of Matter Vocabulary, Describing Material Properties, Objects vs Materials and more.

steps.content: Let's explore States of Matter Vocabulary! Have you ever wondered about this?

Name and distinguish the three states of matter — solid, liquid, and gas — using properties vocabulary: hard, rigid, runny, flows, keeps its shape, fills its container; use 'change of state' to describe what happens when materials are heated or cooled
Let's practice together! Correctly sort a set of everyday materials as solid, liquid, or gas and give a reason for each
If you point to water, ice, and steam from a kettle, can you correctly call each one a liquid, solid, or gas — and use those words naturally?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about States of Matter Vocabulary. Correctly sort a set of everyday materials as solid, liquid, or gas and give a reason for each Use the terms solid, liquid, and gas accurately when describing everyday substances
Have you ever thought about Describing Material Properties? Let's find out together!
Describe simple physical properties of everyday materials such as hard/soft, stretchy/stiff, shiny/dull, rough/smooth, waterproof/absorbent, transparent/opaque
Time to try this out: Use at least five property words correctly: hard, soft, stretchy, shiny, rough, smooth, transparent, waterproof
Can you pick up different objects and describe their properties — like 'this is smooth and shiny' or 'this is rough and bendy'?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Describing Material Properties: Use at least five property words correctly: hard, soft, stretchy, shiny, rough, smooth, transparent, waterproof Describe the properties of three different materials using at least two property words each
Have you ever thought about Objects vs Materials? Let's find out together!
Distinguish between an object and the material from which it is made, understanding that objects can be made from different materials
Time to try this out: Explain the difference between an object (e.g. a chair) and the material it is made from (e.g. wood)
If you hold up a wooden spoon and a metal spoon, can you explain that both are spoons (the object) but made from different materials?

Great job! Today we learned about Objects vs Materials. Explain the difference between an object (e.g. a chair) and the material it is made from (e.g. wood) Give examples of the same object made from different materials (e.g. plastic cup, glass cup)
Let's discover the amazing world of Naming Everyday Materials!
Identify and name a variety of everyday materials including wood, plastic, glass, metal, water, and rock
Time to try this out: Name at least six everyday materials: wood, plastic, glass, metal, water, rock
Can you look around a room and correctly name the materials things are made of — like glass in the window, metal in the radiator, and plastic in a pen?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Naming Everyday Materials: Name at least six everyday materials: wood, plastic, glass, metal, water, rock Identify the material of at least ten different objects around the home or classroom
Today we're going to learn about Grouping Materials. Get ready for an adventure!
Compare and group everyday materials based on their simple physical properties
Let's practice together! Sort a set of materials into groups based on a chosen property (e.g. transparent vs opaque)
Can you sort a pile of objects into groups based on their properties, like all the waterproof ones together or all the transparent ones?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Grouping Materials: Sort a set of materials into groups based on a chosen property (e.g. transparent vs opaque) Compare two materials and state which properties they share and which differ
